name: Denmark
summary: For two Bangladeshi software engineers via a state-approved university, Denmark is strong on the study-to-work arc: acute IT shortage, a 3-year graduate stay with unrestricted work permits, full spouse work rights, and visas decided in Dhaka. The trade-offs are Europe's harshest immigration politics, a top 60.5% tax, and one of the slowest, most exacting citizenship routes (~9 years, B2 Danish) — though dual nationality is allowed.

links.title: New to Denmark (SIRI) – official immigration portal
Study in Denmark – official

job-market.summary: Acute IT shortage; 12 IT titles on the Positive List, both engineers qualify; but Copenhagen-centric and Danish often preferred.
job-market.reasoning: The Positive List for People with a Higher Education (last updated 16/04/2026, ~183 titles) carries 12 IT roles both engineers qualify for: Computer scientist (to 30/06/2027), Programmer and System Developer (to 30/06/2027), Software Backend Developer (to 31/12/2026), IT Architect, Web Developer and more, each needing a 3-year bachelor-level IT education. Denmark reports thousands of unfilled IT roles and a projected graduate shortfall, with strong English-language tech employers in Copenhagen. The January 2026 reforms narrowed the lists and raised pay floors, and demand concentrates in Copenhagen with Danish frequently favoured, capping it below the top tier.
evidence: Positive List (updated 16/04/2026) includes 12 IT titles: Computer scientist, IT Engineer, Programmer and System Developer, IT Architect, System Administrator, Software Backend Developer, Web Developer, IT Consultant, Data Consultant, etc.
Each IT title requires 'at least three years IT education at bachelor level' — both hold a BSc in CSE, so both qualify.
List narrowed from 190 (July 2025) toward ~183 titles for January 2026 as part of broader tightening.
Demand is heavily Copenhagen-centric; salary must match Danish professional standards and be paid to a Danish bank account within 180 days.

links.title: The Positive List for People with a Higher Education (SIRI)
The Pay Limit Scheme (SIRI)

visa-access.summary: Decided in Dhaka: Denmark VFS centre (Gulshan) takes long-stay (D) applications and biometrics on-site; no New Delhi travel, modest funds.
visa-access.reasoning: Bangladeshi nationals lodge both short-stay (C) and long-stay (D) residence-permit applications and give biometrics at the Denmark Visa Application Centre run by VFS Global in Delta Life Tower, Gulshan, Dhaka — the embassy explicitly confirms applicants do not travel to New Delhi. Funds bars are modest (graduate establishment ~DKK 8,231/month, 2025), and the embassy fee is paid online via dys.um.dk. Hurdles are document scrutiny and the January 2026 fee increases (work permit DKK 6,810, accompanying family DKK 3,080), so it scores strong but not best-in-class.
evidence: Denmark Visa Application Centre (VFS Global), Delta Life Tower, Gulshan North, Dhaka 1212, accepts C and D applications with biometrics on site (Sun–Thu 09:00–14:00).
Royal Danish Embassy confirms VFS handles applications locally — no travel to New Delhi required.
Mandatory online embassy fee via dys.um.dk; 2026 fees rose to DKK 6,810 (work permit) and DKK 3,080 (accompanying family).
Graduate job-seeking proof-of-funds ~DKK 8,231/month (2025); student bar ~DKK 7,426/month.

links.title: Royal Danish Embassy Bangladesh – how and where to apply
VFS Global – Denmark visa, Bangladesh

citizenship.summary: Among Europe's hardest: 9 years' residence, Dansk 3 (B2), indfødsretsprøven, self-support, no public debt — but dual allowed since 2015.
citizenship.reasoning: Ordinary naturalisation requires 9 years' continuous residence (reducible to 5 only with 3+ years of substantial Danish education), passing Prøve i Dansk 3 (B2) and the indfødsretsprøven citizenship test, plus self-support (no social assistance in the last 2 years), full-time employment for 3 years 6 months of the last 4 years, no overdue public debt, and a constitution ceremony. Approval also requires inclusion in a twice-yearly parliamentary naturalisation bill, adding 1–2 years. The route is long and exacting, but Denmark has allowed dual citizenship since 2015, so the couple need not renounce Bangladeshi nationality — the one real bright spot.
evidence: 9 years' continuous residence is standard (Dansk 2 only if no social assistance >3 months in 9 years; otherwise Dansk 3 / B2).
Indfødsretsprøven (2021 test) on Danish society, culture and history is mandatory.
Employment 3 years 6 months in the last 4 years, plus no social assistance in the last 2 years and no overdue public debt.
Dual citizenship permitted since 2015; final grant via parliamentary bill twice a year, typically 1–2 years to ceremony.

links.title: Conditions for acquisition of Danish citizenship (borger.dk)
Danish Citizenship (Royal Danish Embassy / um.dk)

post-study-work.summary: 3-year job-search permit after a state-approved Danish degree; unrestricted full-time work permit available, easy Pay Limit switch.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of a state-approved Danish bachelor, master or PhD programme get a residence permit for up to 3 years for job seeking; since 1 April 2023 (replacing the establishment card) they can also obtain a work permit without limitations, lifting the 90-hours/month study-period cap to full-time work anywhere. They can begin working while SIRI processes a job-based permit and switch into the Positive List or Pay Limit Scheme once a qualifying offer lands. This is one of the strongest stay-back regimes for the couple; it sits just below top marks because the later work-permit switch faces 2026 pay floors (Pay Limit DKK 552,000) and the May 2025 cut-offs that exclude non-state-approved programmes.
evidence: Up to 3-year job-seeking permit for graduates of state-approved Danish programmes; fee DKK 3,060, processing ~3 months.
Since 1 April 2023, non-EU graduates can get a work permit without limitations — full-time, any employer (establishment card abolished).
Can start work while a job-based residence-and-work permit is processed.
Non-state-approved (EVA advisory-only) programmes get just a 6-month stay and lost work rights from 2 May 2025.

links.title: Job seeking permit for 3 years after a completed programme (SIRI)
Non-EEA graduates with a 3-year work permit (a-kasser.dk)

spouse-family.summary: Worker's spouse gets FULL work rights; state-approved students keep family + spouse rights (May 2025 cut hit only non-approved courses).
spouse-family.reasoning: Once one partner holds a Pay Limit/Positive List work permit, the accompanying spouse receives a residence-and-work permit with unrestricted employment — ideal as both are software engineers. Crucially, the 2 May 2025 reform that bars spouse residence and work rights applies only to non-state-approved programmes; a couple enrolling at a state-approved university retains spouse and family rights in the study phase too. So if they choose a recognised university (as planned), both can work across study and work phases — a strong outcome, capped by the family-funds bar and 2026 fee rises.
evidence: Accompanying spouse of a worker gets a residence-and-work permit with full labour-market access (SIRI).
May 2025 spouse/family ban applies ONLY to non-state-approved programmes; state-approved university students keep family + spouse rights.
Accompanying-family permit fee rose to DKK 3,080 in 2026; sponsor must show funds for stays of a year or more.
Both engineers can therefore work in both study and work phases on a state-approved route.

links.title: Work permit for accompanying family (SIRI)
New rules for third-country students in non-state-approved programmes (SIRI, May 2025)

msc-study.summary: 350+ English MSc programmes and a 3-year stay-back, but non-EU IT/engineering tuition ~DKK 50–120k/yr with limited scholarships.
msc-study.reasoning: Denmark offers 350+ English-taught programmes and most master's are in English, and a state-approved MSc unlocks the 3-year graduate stay plus retained spouse/family and work rights. The drag is cost: non-EU/EEA master's tuition runs ~DKK 50,000–120,000/yr (DTU IT/engineering at EUR 13,500–17,000), scholarships are limited, and proof-of-funds is ~DKK 7,426/month. Student work is capped at the 90-hours/month study-period limit unless an unrestricted permit is obtained, so cost and limited funding pull this to mid-range.
evidence: 350+ English-taught programmes; non-EU master's tuition ~DKK 50,000–120,000/yr.
DTU (engineering/IT) non-EU tuition ~EUR 13,500–17,000/yr (EUR 7,500/semester).
Standard master's 2 years (120 ECTS); proof-of-funds ~DKK 7,426/month.
State-approved programmes retain full work/family rights; non-state-approved lost these on 2 May 2025.

links.title: Study in Denmark – tuition fees and scholarships
Aarhus University – tuition fees

pr-pathway.summary: 8 years standard, or 4 years if supplementary conditions met — 3.5yr employment, Danish language, self-support, no public debt.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Permanent residence normally requires 8 years' lawful residence, reduced to 4 only when all four supplementary conditions are met: regular employment for at least 3 years 6 months of the last 4 years, a Danish language level, financial self-support with no qualifying benefits or public debt in the last 4 years, and active citizenship/clean record. Applicants must also be employed when SIRI decides. Achievable for two employed engineers on the 4-year track, but condition-heavy and slow, so mid-range.
evidence: 8 years' lawful residence standard; reducible to 4 years if all supplementary conditions are met.
Must have held regular employment at least 3 years 6 months within the last 4 years.
No public debt and no qualifying public benefits in the last 4 years; Danish language and clean-record requirements apply.
Must be employed at the time SIRI decides the application.

links.title: Permanent residence permit (SIRI)
Apply for a permanent residence permit (SIRI)

income-cost.summary: Strong SW pay (median ~DKK 684k, senior 1M+, FAANG higher), but Copenhagen is among the world's priciest cities and tax erodes net.
income-cost.reasoning: Copenhagen software engineers average ~DKK 684,000 total comp (range ~598k–877k), seniors DKK 700k–1.04M and Microsoft/Meta DKK 1.1M–1.9M (Levels.fyi 2026), so two incomes are comfortable. But a city-centre 1-bed runs DKK 12,000–18,000/month and total monthly outlay often exceeds DKK 20,000–22,000 per person, while high tax cuts take-home; the researcher scheme (32.84% for 7 years) lifts net for senior roles. Solid savings potential, not exceptional — mid-range.
evidence: Software engineer average total comp ~DKK 683,806 in Copenhagen; senior DKK 703k–1.04M; Microsoft median ~DKK 1.1M, Meta ~DKK 1.9M (Levels.fyi 2026).
City-centre 1-bed rent DKK 12,000–18,000/month; ~8,000–8,700/month non-rent spend per single.
Copenhagen ranks among the world's most expensive cities; effective tax ~40–52%.
Researcher scheme (32.84% flat for 7 years) materially improves net pay for qualifying senior roles.

links.title: Levels.fyi – Software Engineer, Copenhagen
Cost of living in Copenhagen 2026 (Wise)

healthcare.summary: Universal, tax-funded system free at point of use once a CPR number and yellow health card (Sundhedskort) are issued — covers both spouses.
healthcare.reasoning: Residents staying over three months with a residence permit register for a CPR number and the yellow health card (Sundhedskort), gaining the same free GP, hospital and referred-specialist care as citizens with no premiums or co-pays for most services — covering both spouses from the work or study phase. Emergency care is provided regardless of CPR status. Co-payments remain mainly for adult dental and some prescriptions, hence just below top marks.
evidence: CPR number and yellow card available after staying over 3 months with a residence permit; first card free, replacement DKK 40 (2026).
GP, hospital and referred specialist care free at point of use; GP auto-assigned at registration.
Emergency care provided to anyone regardless of CPR status.
Co-payments persist for adult dental, some prescriptions and optional private/faster care.

links.title: Yellow health card (City of Copenhagen)
Health and safety in Denmark (University of Copenhagen)

culture-language.summary: Very high everyday English in Copenhagen tech; Danish not needed daily but required (Dansk 3 / B2) for citizenship and a level for PR.
culture-language.reasoning: English is pervasive in Copenhagen, in tech workplaces and across 350+ English-taught programmes, so the couple can live and work without Danish initially, and livability is high. The barrier is the long game: Danish at Prøve i Dansk 3 (B2) is mandatory for citizenship and a Danish level is required for permanent residence, so Danish must eventually be learned to complete the migration goals.
evidence: English widely spoken; most master's programmes and much of the tech sector operate in English.
Prøve i Dansk 3 (B2) required for naturalisation (Dansk 2 only if no social assistance >3 months in 9 years).
A Danish language level is also required for permanent residence.
High livability but officialdom and the citizenship endgame are Danish-language gated.

links.title: Conditions for acquisition of Danish citizenship (borger.dk)
Higher educational programmes – study permit (SIRI)

safety-law.summary: Ranked #1 of 143 on the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025; very low crime, low corruption, strong institutional and judicial protections.
safety-law.reasoning: Denmark is the world's top-ranked country on the World Justice Project Rule of Law Index 2025 (1st of 143, ahead of Norway, Finland, Sweden and New Zealand), with very low crime, low corruption and strong, fair institutions. This is a decisive strength for the couple regardless of nationality or religion, even as immigration policy itself is restrictive.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: Denmark ranked 1st of 143 worldwide.
Top five: Denmark (1), Norway (2), Finland (3), Sweden (4), New Zealand (5).
Low crime and corruption with strong judicial fairness and civil-justice scores.
Rule-of-law strength is independent of the country's restrictive migration stance.

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 – global press release
WJP Rule of Law Index – Denmark country profile (PDF)

politics.summary: Very stable democracy, but Europe's most restrictive immigration regime — 'zero refugees', 2026 tightening before a September election.
politics.reasoning: Institutionally Denmark is highly stable, but its immigration stance is among the harshest in Europe, sustained by Social Democrat / centre-right consensus and a 'zero refugees' goal since the 2019 paradigm shift. Asylum approvals hit a historic low (839 by Nov 2025; applications down to 6,735 from 9,645 in 2024), the January 2026 reforms raised fees and pay floors and narrowed job lists, and ministers openly aim to deter foreigners ahead of a September 2026 election. For skilled migrants the work routes persist but the climate is consistently tightening and unwelcoming, so the score is low despite the stable polity.
evidence: Widely described as Europe's most restrictive immigration system (2019 'paradigm shift', 'zero refugees' policy).
1 January 2026 reforms raised fees (work permit DKK 6,810), lifted Pay Limit to DKK 552,000 and narrowed the Positive List.
Asylum approvals at a historic low: 839 by Nov 2025; applications fell from 9,645 (2024) to 6,735 (2025).
Government signals further tightening before the September 2026 general election.

links.title: Denmark records historic-low asylum approvals in 2025
Denmark 2026 residence-permit fees increased (Envoy Global)

tax.summary: Very high: 8% AM-bidrag first, then tax to a top marginal ~60.5% (2026, up from 55.9%); researcher scheme 32.84% for 7yr eases it.
tax.reasoning: Labour income first faces an 8% AM-bidrag, then 12.01% bottom, ~25% municipal, plus middle (7.5% above DKK 696,956), top (7.5% above DKK 845,543) and top-top (5%) taxes, lifting the maximum marginal rate to ~60.5% in 2026 — up from 55.9%, which is why this scores lower than before. The big mitigant for these engineers is the researcher/expat scheme (48E / Forskerordningen): a flat 27% (32.84% with AM-bidrag) for up to 84 months, requiring an average monthly salary of at least DKK 65,400 (2026), which senior software roles can meet. Base burden remains very high, so it stays in the weak band.
evidence: 8% AM-bidrag levied before income tax; municipal ~25.049% average (2026).
Middle tax above DKK 696,956, top tax above DKK 845,543, top-top tax 5% above DKK 2,818,152 (2026).
Maximum marginal rate ~60.5% in 2026, raised from 55.9%.
Researcher scheme (48E): flat 27% / 32.84% with AM-bidrag, up to 84 months, min ~DKK 65,400/month (2026, down from DKK 78,000 in 2025).

links.title: PwC – Denmark individual taxes on personal income
PwC – Denmark individual significant developments (2026)

muslim-diaspora.summary: ~270k Muslims (~4.7%), mosques and halal in cities via Pakistani/Turkish communities; but tiny BD diaspora and 'parallel society' laws.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Denmark has roughly 270,000 Muslims (~4.7%, the second-largest religion), with grand mosques and 100+ mosques and halal food in Copenhagen, Aarhus and Odense, mainly via established Pakistani, Turkish and Arab communities — so practical religious life is feasible in cities. But the specifically Bangladeshi diaspora is very small (~2,000), and a documented hostile-policy trend weighs heavily: 'parallel society' housing laws targeting non-Western-majority areas, the niqab/full-face-covering ban (2018), the ritual-slaughter ban requiring imported halal meat, a ban on foreign mosque funding, and the 2023 'Quran law' debate. Mid-range, leaning cautious.
evidence: ~270,000 Muslims (~4.7%), second-largest religion; grand mosques and 100+ mosques concentrated in Copenhagen, Aarhus, Odense.
Halal and community infrastructure via large Pakistani, Turkish and Arab-origin communities; Bangladeshi diaspora very small (~2,000).
'Parallel society' (former 'ghetto') laws target non-Western-majority neighbourhoods; ECRI urges action against anti-Muslim discrimination.
Niqab/full-face-covering ban (2018), non-stun ritual-slaughter ban (2014), ban on foreign mosque funding, and 2023 'Quran law'.

links.title: Council of Europe (ECRI) – Denmark on anti-Muslim discrimination and 'parallel society'
